lessons.title: Angles, Radians, and the Unit Circle
lessons.content:
Trig starts as geometry but quickly becomes the language of waves. The **unit circle** is a circle of radius $1$ centered at the origin. For an angle $\theta$ measured counterclockwise from the $+x$ axis, the point where the radius meets the circle is $(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)$. So cosine is the $x$-coordinate and sine is the $y$-coordinate — nothing more. Everything else (signs by quadrant, periodicity, the Pythagorean identity) falls out of this picture.

Measure angles in **radians**, not degrees, for any analysis work. One radian is the angle whose arc length equals the radius, so a full turn is $2\pi$ radians $=360^\circ$, giving $1\ \text{rad}=180/\pi\approx57.2958^\circ$. Convert with $\theta_{\text{rad}}=\theta_{\deg}\cdot\pi/180$. Radians are not arbitrary: calculus identities are clean only in radians ($\frac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos x$, and the small-angle rule $\sin x\approx x$), and angular frequency $\omega$ in rad/s drops straight into $\cos(\omega t)$.

Signs follow the quadrant of the point: in QI both $\sin$ and $\cos$ are positive; QII has $\sin>0,\ \cos<0$; QIII both negative; QIV $\cos>0,\ \sin<0$ (the "All Students Take Calculus" mnemonic). Because going around the circle repeats the point, $\sin$ and $\cos$ are periodic with period $2\pi$: $\sin(\theta+2\pi)=\sin\theta$. The single most-used relation, the **Pythagorean identity** $\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1$, is just the equation of the unit circle. As a radar tech you read this circle every time you think about I/Q: $(I,Q)=(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$ on the unit circle is phase.

items.front: What is a radian, and how many are in a full turn?
items.back: The angle subtended at a circle's center by an arc whose length equals the radius. A full turn is $2\pi$ rad $=360^\circ$, so $1\ \text{rad}=180/\pi\approx57.2958^\circ$.

items.front: On the unit circle, how do $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ relate to a point?
items.back: For an angle $\theta$ measured CCW from the $+x$ axis, the point on the unit circle is $(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)$. Cosine is the $x$-coordinate, sine is the $y$-coordinate.

items.front: State the Pythagorean identity and explain why it holds.
items.back: $\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1$. It is simply the equation of the unit circle $x^2+y^2=1$ with $x=\cos\theta,\ y=\sin\theta$.

items.front: Why do engineers use radians instead of degrees for analysis?
items.back: Calculus is clean only in radians: $\frac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos x$ and $\sin x\approx x$ for small $x$. Angular frequency $\omega$ (rad/s) plugs directly into $\cos(\omega t)$. Degrees force stray $\pi/180$ factors.

items.front: What are the signs of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ in each quadrant?
items.back: QI: both $+$. QII: $\sin>0,\ \cos<0$. QIII: both $-$. QIV: $\cos>0,\ \sin<0$. Mnemonic: All, Sin, Tan, Cos are the positive functions in QI–QIV.

items.explanation: $2\pi/3=120^\circ$ is in QII (cosine negative). Its reference angle is $60^\circ$, so $\cos=-\cos 60^\circ=-1/2$.

items.prompt: A point on the unit circle is $(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)=(-0.6,\,0.8)$. Which quadrant is $\theta$ in?
items.explanation: $x=\cos\theta<0$ and $y=\sin\theta>0$ means QII. (Check: $0.6^2+0.8^2=1$, a valid unit-circle point.)

items.prompt: Using the small-angle approximation, $\sin(0.02)\ \text{rad}$ is closest to:
items.explanation: For small $x$ in radians, $\sin x\approx x$, so $\sin(0.02)\approx0.02$. This only works in radians.

lessons.title: The Six Functions, Graphs, and Inverses
lessons.content:
Six functions, but really two: define $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ on the unit circle, and the rest are ratios. $\tan\theta=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$, and the reciprocals are $\csc\theta=1/\sin\theta$, $\sec\theta=1/\cos\theta$, $\cot\theta=1/\tan\theta$. Tangent and secant blow up wherever $\cos\theta=0$ (at $\theta=\pi/2+k\pi$), producing vertical asymptotes.

The graphs: $\sin$ and $\cos$ are bounded in $[-1,1]$ with period $2\pi$; they are the same wave shifted, $\cos\theta=\sin(\theta+\pi/2)$. $\sin$ is **odd** ($\sin(-\theta)=-\sin\theta$) and $\cos$ is **even** ($\cos(-\theta)=\cos\theta$). Tangent has period $\pi$ (not $2\pi$) and ranges over all reals. A general sinusoid $y=A\sin(B\theta+C)+D$ has amplitude $|A|$, period $2\pi/|B|$, phase shift $-C/B$, and vertical offset $D$ — the four knobs you will turn constantly in signal work.

**Inverse functions** undo these, but only on restricted domains so they stay single-valued. $\arcsin$ returns values in $[-\pi/2,\pi/2]$, $\arccos$ in $[0,\pi]$, and $\arctan$ in $(-\pi/2,\pi/2)$. That restriction is exactly why a naive $\arctan(y/x)$ loses quadrant information: it cannot tell $(1,1)$ from $(-1,-1)$. Software fixes this with **`atan2(y, x)`**, which takes both arguments separately, inspects their signs, and returns the full-range angle in $(-\pi,\pi]$. For any phase computation from I/Q samples — $\phi=\operatorname{atan2}(Q,I)$ — you want `atan2`, never plain `arctan`. This is the single most common silent bug when porting phase math into code, alongside feeding degrees to a function that expects radians.

items.front: Define the six trig functions in terms of $\sin$ and $\cos$.
items.back: $\tan\theta=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$, $\cot\theta=\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}$, $\sec\theta=\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$, $\csc\theta=\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$. Only $\sin$ and $\cos$ are primitive; the rest are ratios.
items.hint: Tangent is sine over cosine; the others are reciprocals.

items.front: What are the periods of $\sin$, $\cos$, and $\tan$?
items.back: $\sin$ and $\cos$ have period $2\pi$. $\tan$ has period $\pi$ because $\tan(\theta+\pi)=\tan\theta$.
items.hint: Two of them match; tangent is half.

items.front: Which trig functions are even and which are odd?
items.back: $\cos$ is even: $\cos(-\theta)=\cos\theta$. $\sin$ and $\tan$ are odd: $\sin(-\theta)=-\sin\theta$, $\tan(-\theta)=-\tan\theta$.

items.front: For $y=A\sin(B\theta+C)+D$, name the amplitude, period, phase shift, and offset.
items.back: Amplitude $=|A|$; period $=2\pi/|B|$; phase shift $=-C/B$ (positive $C$ shifts left); vertical offset $=D$.
items.hint: Four knobs: height, width, slide, lift.

items.front: Why does software use `atan2(y, x)` instead of `arctan(y/x)`?
items.back: `arctan` returns only $(-\pi/2,\pi/2)$ and loses the quadrant because it sees one ratio. `atan2(y,x)` uses the signs of both arguments to return the correct full-range angle in $(-\pi,\pi]$ — essential for phase from I/Q.
items.hint: One ratio can't distinguish opposite quadrants.

items.explanation: Period $=2\pi/|B|=2\pi/4=\pi/2$. The amplitude $3$ does not affect period.

items.prompt: What is the range (set of output values) of $\arccos(x)$?
items.explanation: $\arccos$ is restricted to $[0,\pi]$ to stay single-valued. ($\arcsin$ uses $[-\pi/2,\pi/2]$; $\arctan$ uses $(-\pi/2,\pi/2)$.)

items.prompt: An I/Q sample has $I=-1,\ Q=-1$. What does $\operatorname{atan2}(Q,I)$ return?
items.explanation: Both components negative → QIII. `atan2(-1,-1)` $=-3\pi/4$ (equivalently $225^\circ$). Plain $\arctan(-1/-1)=\arctan(1)=\pi/4$ would be wrong by $\pi$.

lessons.title: Sinusoids in Engineering Form & Phase
lessons.content:
Engineers write a sinusoid as $x(t)=A\cos(\omega t+\varphi)$: amplitude $A$, **angular frequency** $\omega=2\pi f$ in rad/s, and phase $\varphi$ in radians. The period is $T=1/f=2\pi/\omega$. A phase shift is a time shift: $A\cos(\omega t+\varphi)=A\cos\!\big(\omega(t+\varphi/\omega)\big)$, so a delay $\Delta t$ corresponds to a phase $\varphi=-\omega\,\Delta t=-2\pi f\,\Delta t$ (a delay is a phase *lag*). This phase–delay duality is the entire basis of beamforming: steering a phased array is just applying the right per-element phase to match a wavefront's geometric delay.

The key algebraic skill is collapsing a sine-plus-cosine into one sinusoid. Given $a\cos(\omega t)+b\sin(\omega t)$, write it as $R\cos(\omega t+\varphi)$ where
$$R=\sqrt{a^2+b^2},\qquad \varphi=\operatorname{atan2}(-b,\,a).$$
Expand $R\cos(\omega t+\varphi)=R\cos\varphi\cos\omega t-R\sin\varphi\sin\omega t$ and match: $a=R\cos\varphi$, $b=-R\sin\varphi$. For example, $3\cos\omega t+4\sin\omega t$ gives $R=\sqrt{9+16}=5$ and $\varphi=\operatorname{atan2}(-4,3)\approx-53.13^\circ$, so it equals $5\cos(\omega t-53.13^\circ)$.

A worked phase example you will use in radar: a $1\,\text{ns}$ delay at $1\,\text{GHz}$. Phase $=2\pi f\,\Delta t=2\pi(10^9)(10^{-9})=2\pi$ rad $=360^\circ$ — a full cycle. Same delay at $250\,\text{MHz}$ is $90^\circ$. This is why the same cable length means very different phase at different carriers, and why phase, not delay, is the natural variable on the unit circle.

items.front: In $x(t)=A\cos(\omega t+\varphi)$, what are $\omega$ and $T$?
items.back: $\omega=2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in rad/s; the period $T=1/f=2\pi/\omega$. $A$ is amplitude and $\varphi$ is the phase in radians.

items.front: How does a time delay $\Delta t$ relate to phase for a sinusoid of frequency $f$?
items.back: $\varphi=-\omega\,\Delta t=-2\pi f\,\Delta t$. A delay produces a phase *lag* (negative phase). Higher frequency → more phase for the same delay.

items.front: Convert $a\cos(\omega t)+b\sin(\omega t)$ to amplitude–phase form.
items.back: $R\cos(\omega t+\varphi)$ with $R=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ and $\varphi=\operatorname{atan2}(-b,a)$. Derivation: match $a=R\cos\varphi,\ b=-R\sin\varphi$.

items.front: Express $3\cos\omega t+4\sin\omega t$ as a single cosine.
items.back: $R=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5$, $\varphi=\operatorname{atan2}(-4,3)\approx-53.13^\circ$. So it equals $5\cos(\omega t-53.13^\circ)$.

items.front: Why does the same cable length cause different phase at different carrier frequencies?
items.back: Phase $=2\pi f\,\Delta t$ is proportional to $f$ for a fixed delay $\Delta t$. Doubling the carrier doubles the phase shift, even though the physical delay is unchanged.

items.prompt: What phase shift (in degrees) does a $1\,\text{ns}$ delay produce at $1\,\text{GHz}$?
items.explanation: $\varphi=2\pi f\,\Delta t=2\pi(10^9)(10^{-9})=2\pi$ rad $=360^\circ$ — one full cycle.

items.prompt: Written as $R\cos(\omega t+\varphi)$, what is $R$ for $-5\cos\omega t+12\sin\omega t$?
A signal $\cos(\omega t)$ is delayed by a quarter period. The result is:
items.explanation: A quarter-period delay is $\Delta t=T/4$, so $\varphi=-\omega T/4=-\pi/2$. $\cos(\omega t-\pi/2)=\sin(\omega t)$ — a delayed cosine lags by $90^\circ$ and becomes a sine.

items.prompt: A waveform has $\omega=2\pi\cdot 60\ \text{rad/s}$. What is its period $T$?

lessons.title: Identities, Mixing, and Triangle Solving
lessons.content:
The identities are not trivia — each one is a tool. The **sum/difference** identities, $\cos(A\pm B)=\cos A\cos B\mp\sin A\sin B$ and $\sin(A\pm B)=\sin A\cos B\pm\cos A\sin B$, generate everything else. Setting $A=B$ gives the **double-angle** forms $\sin 2A=2\sin A\cos A$ and $\cos 2A=\cos^2A-\sin^2A=1-2\sin^2A$, and solving the latter for the squared term gives the **half-angle/power-reduction** identity $\sin^2A=\tfrac12(1-\cos 2A)$, which you use whenever you average power.

The **product-to-sum** identities are literally how a mixer works. Multiply two tones and
$$\cos A\cos B=\tfrac12\big[\cos(A-B)+\cos(A+B)\big].$$
If $A=\omega_1 t$ and $B=\omega_2 t$, the product yields a **difference frequency** $\omega_1-\omega_2$ and a **sum frequency** $\omega_1+\omega_2$ — exactly the IF and image a heterodyne receiver produces. A low-pass filter then keeps the difference term. That single identity explains superheterodyne radar and radio front ends.

For geometry — antenna array spacing, radar triangulation — use the **law of sines** $\dfrac{a}{\sin A}=\dfrac{b}{\sin B}=\dfrac{c}{\sin C}$ and the **law of cosines** $c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos C$. The law of cosines is the Pythagorean theorem with a correction term; when $C=90^\circ$, $\cos C=0$ and it collapses to $c^2=a^2+b^2$. Use the law of cosines when you know two sides and the included angle (or all three sides); use the law of sines for angle-side-angle situations, watching for the ambiguous (SSA) case.

items.front: State the cosine and sine sum/difference identities.
items.back: $\cos(A\pm B)=\cos A\cos B\mp\sin A\sin B$; $\sin(A\pm B)=\sin A\cos B\pm\cos A\sin B$. Note the sign flip in the cosine form.
items.hint: Cosine: like-terms minus; sine: cross-terms plus.

items.front: Give the three forms of $\cos 2A$.
items.back: $\cos 2A=\cos^2A-\sin^2A=1-2\sin^2A=2\cos^2A-1$. All follow from the sum identity with $A=B$ plus $\cos^2+\sin^2=1$.
items.hint: One uses both squares; the other two use just one.

items.front: Write the product-to-sum identity for $\cos A\cos B$ and tie it to mixing.
items.back: $\cos A\cos B=\tfrac12[\cos(A-B)+\cos(A+B)]$. With $A=\omega_1 t,\ B=\omega_2 t$, multiplying two tones creates sum ($\omega_1+\omega_2$) and difference ($\omega_1-\omega_2$) frequencies — exactly heterodyne mixing.

items.front: State the power-reduction identity for $\sin^2A$ and why it matters.
items.back: $\sin^2A=\tfrac12(1-\cos 2A)$. The DC part $\tfrac12$ is what survives time-averaging, so the average power of a unit sinusoid is $1/2$.
items.hint: Average of the oscillating $\cos 2A$ term is zero.

items.front: State the law of cosines and its relation to the Pythagorean theorem.
items.back: $c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos C$, where $C$ is the angle opposite side $c$. When $C=90^\circ$, $\cos C=0$ and it reduces to $c^2=a^2+b^2$.

items.prompt: Two tones at $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are multiplied in a mixer. Which frequencies appear?
items.explanation: By product-to-sum, $\cos\omega_1 t\cos\omega_2 t=\tfrac12[\cos(\omega_1-\omega_2)t+\cos(\omega_1+\omega_2)t]$ — sum and difference frequencies.

items.prompt: A triangle has sides $a=5$, $b=7$ with included angle $C=60^\circ$. Find side $c$.
Which scenario is the ambiguous case where the law of sines may give two triangles?
items.explanation: SSA (two sides and a non-included angle) can correspond to zero, one, or two valid triangles — the classic ambiguous case for the law of sines.
